title: Beeswax furniture polish
subtitle: A traditional beeswax and lemon oil furniture polish for bare and oiled wood surfaces
excerpt: Beeswax mixed with a light carrier oil produces a paste polish that feeds wood fibres and leaves a warm, low-sheen finish. This recipe uses lemon oil as both carrier and fragrance, giving the polish a clean citrus scent. Apply sparingly, allow to haze, and buff with a dry cloth. Not suitable for high-gloss lacquered surfaces.
sourceNotes: Beeswax furniture polish: traditional beeswax and oil formulation. Lemon oil (food-grade mineral oil infused with lemon peel, or pure lemon essential oil in a carrier) as the liquid fraction. Beeswax ratio approximately 30 to 40% wax to 60 to 70% oil. Melt wax over double boiler; pour into tin to set. Suitable for bare, oiled, and waxed wood; not lacquered finishes.

recipe.batchNotes: Scale proportionally. Pour into multiple tins.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Allow to set for at least 1 hour before use.
recipe.temperatureNote: Melt beeswax to 65 to 70°C. Remove from heat before adding oil.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: 2 years at room temperature in a sealed tin. Beeswax is very stable. If the polish becomes too hard in cold weather, warm the tin briefly in a bowl of hot water.

glossaryTerms.definition: A blend of beeswax and oil applied to wood surfaces. Beeswax fills and seals the grain, and forms a water-resistant surface layer. The oil keeps the wax pliable and feeds the wood fibres.
Applying firm circular pressure with a dry cloth after the polish haze has formed. Buffing removes excess wax and brings up the shine by smoothing the wax layer.

content.text: is one of the oldest wood-care products in common use. This recipe is a 35:65 ratio of beeswax to lemon oil, which gives a soft paste at room temperature. Apply a thin layer, allow it to haze (about 10 minutes), then
brings up a warm sheen. Do not use on high-gloss lacquered or varnished furniture; the wax cannot penetrate and will leave smears. Suitable for bare wood, oiled wood, and previously waxed surfaces.
Melt beeswax over a simmering-water bath only. Never leave melting wax unattended. Keep a kitchen-fire blanket within reach.

items.prepNote: Food-grade lemon-scented mineral oil or sweet almond oil with lemon EO; 67% of blend
Optional; add if using unscented carrier oil; add off heat

content.text: Melt the beeswax in the pouring pitcher over a simmering-water bath to 65 to 70°C. Remove from heat. Add the lemon oil and stir to combine. If using lemon essential oil, add it at this stage. Stir well.
Pour carefully into a metal tin. Leave at room temperature for at least 1 hour to set to a soft paste. Do not refrigerate; the polish becomes too hard.

Apply a small amount with a soft cloth or natural-bristle brush. Work in the direction of the wood grain. Allow to haze for 10 minutes, then buff firmly with a clean dry cloth. A little polish goes a long way; applying too thickly leaves a sticky residue.
2 years in a sealed tin at room temperature. If the polish sets very hard in cold weather, warm the tin in a bowl of hot water for a few minutes.
